Tonight's guest wishes to remain anonymous
And with that in mind, I'm just going to call her Kendra.
Kendra, welcome to the show.
It's good to be here, Vic.
Thank you for having me.
Well, you know, you're welcome.
Thanks for your time.
Kendra, please give us a brief bio on yourself.
I'm about 54 years old.
I have three grown, wonderful children that live all over the place.
I have three wonderful dogs and a cat.
Sounds like you've carved a good life out for yourself.
I sure try, Vic.
Well, that's great. That's how it should be.
Kendra, you were pretty upset the first time we spoke about your encounter, but where are you now from a mental standpoint?
I really feel a lot better about all of this since I talked to you.
I mean, I hadn't really talked about it. It's been nearly five years.
I didn't even realize how bad I felt about it and how still scared I was every day.
I can tell you I still think about those glowing eyes every day, but at least they're not as
poignant as it was.
They're kind of fading now.
I'm not ready to go walking in the woods by myself yet, but maybe I can.
So that's a lot better than I was.
Well, like I told you, in good time.
I think you will get there, but please don't forget to take baby steps.
There's no rush.
The woods aren't going to go anywhere.
They're going to be there.
I need to think of it more like that too, but I hope to be able to take the dogs for
a walk down there.
Well, I hope you can too.
It's coming.
It's just going to take time.
You mentioned that it's been about five years since you've even talked about that encounter.
I know that you told two people about the experience, but who were they?
My son was just, I actually only told him maybe about a month ago. My boyfriend, though,
I called him right after the experience because I was totally freaking out. It was really,
really early in the morning too. So I knew he was still up, but this was during COVID and
I would say late January of 2020 while driving to work. And recently, I actually told my mom
about it on Thanksgiving. But before that, nobody knew, only my boyfriend and I for years,
until very recently, within the last month or so, two months.
Well, it's not easy to talk about an experience like that. I get it. Do you think the isolation
you experienced during the pandemic is one of the reasons why you had so much trouble dealing with
that encounter? Probably. I mean, when I was working at the quarry, that was considered to be
you had to go to work. I wasn't off any of the time that everybody else was off for COVID. I
went to work every day. They were considered essential due to the maintenance of roads,
basically. So I was an essential worker. I actually carried a letter in my car just in
case I got pulled over that said, yes, I'm supposed to be out on the road.
I am an essential worker.
But thinking about how dark it was there and even at the quarry where I worked,
that was on my mind quite a bit.
Oh, I'm sure it was.

I bet it was on your mind all the time Did your job there at the stone quarry require you to work alone in remote areas out there Yes I was in the scale house which was not attached to the office It was on the actual
opposite side of the quarry. So like management was over at the one office near the river. And
then I was all the way in the back, closer to the woods, actually. The woods surround the quarry,
except for where it's on the river. But there's several hundred acres that are involved in that.
Yeah. And I would have to, I was supposed to be at work by 5.30 at that time. And then they
actually made it closer to five. So I was leaving for work at like 4.30 in the morning. It was a
good 45 minute drive, but I always, you know, left myself a little time in case something happened,
you know, or I wanted to stop and get a coffee or something. I tried to build 15 minutes into
my commute just so in case something like maybe, I don't know, maybe I got pulled over. I mean,
hope I didn't have that or a flat tire or something like that. I still do it now too.
Yeah, that couldn't have been easy at all. And speaking of not easy, almost any scale house
I've ever seen at a place like a quarry was just nothing but windows. Is that how yours was?
Yes, it was all windows.
Actually, that's funny you say that because I used to think that I was almost like a zoo animal.
Like everybody could just see in.
I didn't necessarily be able to see anybody out there in the dark.
But yeah, and it was really dark then because it was winter.
So the sun wasn't coming up to, what, seven, something like that.
And I would work until 4.30, 5 o'clock every day.
So it was a long day for sure.
And it was dark.
And there was a lot of wildlife there.
A huge amount of wildlife, actually.
Wow, you poor thing.
Yeah, that couldn't have been easy.
I'm sure it goes without saying you kept that door locked, didn't you?

That door was locked 24-7.

Of course, management had a key, but they would knock.

But, you know, obviously you don't want windows in there, really.
No, definitely you wouldn't.

Did you ever do anything to try and lessen the difficulty of doing that?
Or were you just stuck having to do it with no relief?
I was stuck having to do it with no relief.

At least just nothing else for the company.

Not that they could protect me from one of them, but maybe just having somebody with me would have made me feel better.
It got awful lonely in there, especially during COVID.
It wasn't very busy.
So there were long days just sitting there all day by myself.
Wow.
Yeah, that's not easy at all.
I feel for you.
I really do.

I mean, really, it was quite terrifying.
